next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

Doug folded the same flannel shirt for the third time. The sleeves wouldn't square right, the cuffs refusing to line up, and he gave up and stuffed it into his duffel. His other shirts were already in there, along with the prescription bottles he'd moved from the upstairs bathroom Saturday morning — the Lexapro and the Ativan, buried between a pair of jeans and his shaving kit, where they'd been since this whole thing started. He'd taken his evening dose an hour ago in the basement bathroom with the door locked, the way he'd taken every dose this weekend: quickly, quietly, the bottle opened and closed in a single motion so that the rattle of pills didn't carry through the thin walls. Twenty-five years of friendship and he still couldn't swallow an antidepressant without making sure the coast was clear.

The great room had the feel of a waiting room where the appointment had been cancelled. Paul sat at the kitchen table with his reading glasses and his phone, texting Anne about departure times, his thumbs moving with the deliberate patience of a man who answered every text in complete sentences. Joel had his laptop on the couch, answering the work emails he'd been ignoring since Thursday, the blue light catching the sharp angles of his face. The draft board hung on the wall above the fireplace. Martin's picks were still circled in red marker, and nobody had suggested taking it down. Nobody had suggested much of anything today. The day had passed in the half-speed of an airport delay — too awake to sleep, too tired to do anything useful, suspended in the lag bet...
open_hooks_to_carry: The fantasy football waiver wire debate: should Martin's auto-drafted team stand? Who manages his roster as tribute?
The argument becomes a proxy for loyalty, obligation, and what they owe each other and the dead
Nate observes: Tommy and Cliff sitting too far apart and being too polite
A last dinner, a sunset on the lake — the final moment that feels like the weekends that came before
This chapter must land as genuinely funny while the reader senses, consciously or not, that the comedy is about to end
prev_tail_excerpt:
...rrows showing where a body could go if someone told it to. He didn't push it away. He didn't draw it up. He let it sit there the way you let a bad idea sit on the board during a late practice, the kind of idea you're supposed to erase before the players see it, except Tommy didn't erase it because it wasn't a bad idea. It was a simple idea. It was the kind of idea Tommy was built for — physical, direct, one step, done.

The loon called again from somewhere out past the dock, and the lake had gone from copper to the flat gray it turned when the sun dropped behind the far tree line — the color change so gradual you couldn't point to the moment it happened, but the result was definitive. The golden afternoon was gone. September nights in Minnesota started warm and ended cold. You could go from shirtsleeves to shivering in an hour. The deck planks got slick when the temperature dropped and the dew settled. A man in leather loafers on wet wood in the dark could lose his footing. Might not even make a sound that carried eighty yards through pine and birch to the house, especially with the loons calling and the refrigerator running and nobody listening for anything because why would anyone be listening.

Tommy picked up the beer Cliff had brought him. The can was still full. He held it for a moment, then tipped it over the rail and watched the liquid catch the last gray light for a second before it hit the ground and disappeared into the pine needles below the deck.

He set the empty can on the armrest where Cliff had put it. He wiped his hands on his shorts.

Then he went inside.
secondary_character_beats: Doug should be drinking water instead of beer — a small detail that matters later
Joel and Paul should exchange a look that signals they are beginning to compare notes
state_deltas_required: the group's desperate performance of normalcy while every relationship is fractured
the group's pretense of normalcy is audibly cracking — Nate observes the Tommy-Cliff tension — last comedy before the break
